Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LP(MP)A 1989 s.2, what is the essential requirement for a valid contract for sale of land?

options.A: The contract must be in writing and signed by both parties, incorporating all material terms.
options.B: The contract must be in writing and signed by the seller only.
options.C: The contract must be in writing but need not identify all material terms if agreed orally.
options.D: The contract must be signed by both parties but can be in electronic format only.

Property Practice.explanation: LP(MP)A 1989 s.2 requires that a contract for sale of land must be in writing and signed by or on behalf of both parties. All material terms must be incorporated into the written document. Part-performance is no longer available to avoid this requirement post-s.2.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LRA 2002, what is the basic position regarding the priority of registered dispositions?

options.A: Registered dispositions take priority in accordance with the order of registration, subject to overriding interests.
options.B: Later registered dispositions always take priority over earlier unregistered interests regardless of notice.
options.C: Priority is determined by the order in which parties became aware of earlier interests.
options.D: Unregistered interests always take priority if the owner had actual occupation.

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 s.29 provides that a registrable disposition for valuable consideration takes priority according to the order of registration. However, overriding interests under Sch.3 (such as actual occupation under para 2) can take priority despite not being registered.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Which search is most likely to reveal whether a commercial lease is subject to forfeiture proceedings?

options.A: Local Authority Search.
options.B: Water and Drainage Search.
options.C: Bankruptcy Search against the landlord.
options.D: None of the standard searches — this requires enquiry of the landlord and review of lease terms.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard searches (Local Authority, Water, Drainage, Chancel Repair) do not reveal forfeiture proceedings. This information must be obtained by: (1) reviewing the lease terms and conditions; (2) enquiring of the current occupier about breaches; (3) reviewing notices served; and (4) conducting company searches if the landlord is a company in financial distress.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: What is the SDLT rate payable on the purchase of a residential property for £250,000 by a first-time buyer with no previous property interest?

options.A: 1% on the amount over £125,000.
options.B: 2% on the whole amount.
options.C: 5% on the whole amount.
options.D: 0% — fully relieved as first-time buyer relief.

Property Practice.explanation: First-time buyer relief under FA 2003 (as amended) provides full relief on residential property up to £425,000 for first-time buyers. For purchases at £250,000, a first-time buyer is fully relieved of SDLT. Higher rates (+3%) apply only to additional residential properties for non-first-time buyers.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under the Standard Conditions of Sale (6th edition), what is the default completion time frame after exchange of contracts?

options.A: 10 working days.
options.B: 14 calendar days.
options.C: 5 working days.
options.D: The contract specifies the precise date; the standard conditions do not set a default period.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard Condition 6.1.1 provides that completion takes place on the date specified in the contract. Absent express agreement, the default position is 5 working days after exchange. The completion date is critical and must be agreed in writing before exchange.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LRA 2002 Sch.3 para 2, what TWO conditions must be satisfied for an interest based on actual occupation to override a registered disposition?

options.A: The owner must be in actual occupation and their interest must be known to the buyer OR would have been obvious on reasonable inspection.
options.B: The owner must be in occupation and have paid council tax.
options.C: The owner must be in occupation for at least 12 months and have paid rent.
options.D: The owner must be in occupation and must have a written notice of the interest.

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 Sch.3 para 2 requires: (1) the person claiming the interest is in actual occupation at the time of the disposition; and (2) the interest is known to the buyer, or would have been obvious on reasonable inspection. Both conditions must be satisfied. Actual occupation alone is insufficient if the interest would not have been apparent.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for a business tenant, Wharton Tech Ltd, occupying office premises under a five-year lease that expires on 30 June 2025. The landlord has not served any notice under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954. Wharton Tech continues to trade from the premises after 30 June 2025. Under LTA 1954 s.24, what is the legal effect on the tenancy after the contractual term ends?

options.A: The tenancy continues automatically by operation of statute until terminated by either party following the LTA 1954 notice procedure.
options.B: The tenancy immediately terminates and Wharton Tech must vacate or become a trespasser.
options.C: The tenancy converts automatically into a periodic tenancy with a new contractual term.
options.D: The tenancy continues but only until the landlord demands vacant possession by letter.

Property Practice.explanation: Option A is correct. LTA 1954 s.24(1) provides that a business tenancy to which Part II applies shall not expire at the end of its contractual term but shall continue until terminated in accordance with the Act. Termination requires either: the landlord serving a s.25 notice (at least 6 months' notice, specifying whether the landlord will oppose renewal); or the tenant serving a s.26 request for a new tenancy; or the tenant serving a s.27 notice to quit. A landlord cannot simply demand vacant possession by informal letter. Option B is wrong: without a valid s.25 notice, the tenancy continues beyond the contractual term; Wharton Tech does not become a trespasser. Option C is wrong: the tenancy does not automatically convert — it remains the original tenancy held over by statute. Option D is wrong: an informal demand is ineffective — the LTA 1954 notice procedure is mandatory.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: When does the mortgagee's power of sale under LPA 1925 s.101 become exercisable?

options.A: Immediately upon execution of the mortgage deed.
options.B: When three months' rent is in arrears or the mortgagor breaches any term.
options.C: When capital is 3 months overdue OR interest is 2 months arrears OR any covenant is breached.
options.D: Only after the mortgagee obtains a court order.

Property Practice.explanation: LPA 1925 s.103 specifies when the power of sale becomes exercisable: (1) when capital money is due and unpaid for at least 3 months after becoming due; (2) when interest is 2 months in arrears; or (3) when a covenant of the mortgage is breached. The power arises under s.101 but does not become exercisable until one of these conditions is met.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under the UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook, when must a solicitor disclose a conflict of interest arising from acting for both lender and borrower?

options.A: Immediately upon discovery of any matter that could affect the lender's position or create a conflict.
options.B: Only if the lender specifically requests disclosure.
options.C: Only if the borrower is purchasing at a significant undervalue compared to the valuation.
options.D: Never — the Handbook permits joint representation without disclosure if the borrower consents.

Property Practice.explanation: The UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook requires solicitors acting for both lender and borrower to disclose conflicts immediately. Key conflict situations: (1) undervalue purchases; (2) non-disclosure to lender; (3) undisclosed incentives or gifts. Immediate disclosure is required; in serious conflicts, the solicitor should decline to act for one party.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under POCA 2002 s.330, when must a solicitor submit a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency?

options.A: As soon as reasonably practicable after knowing or suspecting money laundering, before proceeding with the transaction.
options.B: Only if the solicitor is certain that money laundering has occurred.
options.C: Within 7 days of forming a suspicion of money laundering.
options.D: Only if the client refuses to provide satisfactory explanation for the source of funds.

Property Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.330 requires disclosure of any knowledge or suspicion of money laundering to the National Crime Agency. The disclosure must be made as soon as reasonably practicable and must be made before proceeding with the transaction. Failure to disclose (without appropriate consent) constitutes a criminal offence. The threshold is suspicion, not certainty.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A property investor purchases a second residential property (not their main residence) for £350,000. There are no reliefs available. What SDLT is payable?

options.A: £14,250 (graduated rates plus 3% higher rate surcharge).
options.C: £13,250 (3% higher rate on the whole amount).
options.D: £15,000 (standard graduated rates at standard residential rates only).

Property Practice.explanation: For a second residential property, the higher rate of 3% applies (in addition to standard rates). Calculation: £0-£125k at 0% = £0; £125k-£250k at 5% = £6,250; £250k-£350k at 5% + 3% higher rate = £7,500 at higher rate = £14,250 total SDLT.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A buyer takes a registrable disposition (legal estate) for valuable consideration. An unregistered equitable interest in the same property was created before the disposition but was not overridden. Which statement best explains the priority position?

options.A: The registered disposition always takes priority because it is registered.
options.B: The unregistered equitable interest takes priority because it was created first.
options.C: Both interests have equal status and must be dealt with by contribution agreement.
options.D: The registered disposition takes priority unless the equitable interest is a Sch.3 overriding interest (e.g. actual occupation).

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 s.29 provides that a registrable disposition for valuable consideration takes free of all unregistered interests EXCEPT those listed in Sch.3 (overriding interests). Sch.3 para 2 (actual occupation) is the most common exception. If the unregistered equitable owner is in actual occupation and the interest was known to the buyer or obvious on inspection, it overrides the registered disposition.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Two dispositions of the same registered land are presented for registration on the same day: a first mortgage and a second charge. Both are correct in form. In what order should they be registered, and what is the priority outcome?

options.A: They can be registered in any order; priority is determined by date of execution.
options.B: The first to be presented for registration is registered first and takes priority.
options.C: The first mortgage should be registered first (as it provides security); the second charge has no priority rights.
options.D: Both must be registered in the order of presentation; priority is determined by this order regardless of the charge structure.

Property Practice.explanation: Dispositions are registered in the order in which they are presented for registration. Both charges would be registered in order of presentation, with the first registered charge taking priority over the second. The character of the charge (mortgage vs second charge) does not alter the registration order rule — it is simply order of presentation that determines priority under LRA 2002.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A property is held on trust by A and B (trustees) for C (beneficiary with a beneficial interest in the equitable estate). The property is sold to a buyer. For C's interest to be overreached, which condition must be met?

options.A: The buyer must conduct a search showing the trust on the register.
options.B: The buyer must have actual notice of the trust.
options.C: C's interest must be disclosed to the buyer in writing.
options.D: Capital money from the sale must be paid to BOTH trustees (or a trust corporation).

Property Practice.explanation: Overreaching under LPA 1925 ss.2 and 27 requires: (1) sale by at least two trustees (or a trust corporation); and (2) capital money paid to the trustees. If these conditions are met, even a buyer with notice of the trust takes the legal estate free of the beneficiary's equitable interest — the interest is shifted to the capital money. Failure to pay two trustees means the interest is NOT overreached.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A tenant under a commercial lease with a forfeiture clause is three months in arrears with rent. The landlord wishes to forfeit the lease. What procedural step must the landlord take BEFORE peaceable re-entry?

options.A: Serve a s.146 LPA 1925 notice specifying the breach and giving a reasonable time to remedy.
options.B: For non-payment of rent only, no notice is required — peaceable re-entry is permitted immediately.
options.C: Obtain a court order for forfeiture.
options.D: Serve a forfeiture notice via registered post and wait 14 days.

Property Practice.explanation: For non-payment of rent specifically, s.146 LPA 1925 does NOT require notice before forfeiture (this is the exception to the general s.146 notice requirement). However, for other breaches, s.146 notice is mandatory. If the breach is remediable, the tenant must be given a reasonable opportunity to remedy. For straightforward arrears of rent, a landlord can proceed to peaceable re-entry or court forfeiture without prior s.146 notice.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A landlord wishes to oppose the grant of a new business tenancy under LTA 1954 on the ground that the landlord intends to demolish and rebuild the property. What must the landlord establish?

options.A: The landlord must have a firm intention to demolish; it need not be certain or immediate.
options.B: The landlord must prove that demolition is reasonably likely within a reasonable time after the tenancy ends.
options.C: The landlord must demonstrate that demolition cannot be carried out without possession of the demised property.
options.D: The landlord must obtain planning permission for redevelopment before opposing renewal.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.30(1)(f) permits opposition to renewal where the landlord intends to demolish or reconstruct AND that demolition/reconstruction cannot be carried out without possession. The landlord must establish: (1) firm intention to demolish/reconstruct; (2) reasonable expectation this will occur within a reasonable time; and (3) that the works cannot be undertaken without obtaining possession of the property. The leading case is Espresso Coffee Machine Co v Aintree plc.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LTA 1954 s.30(1)(e), a landlord opposes renewal on the ground of persistent substantial breaches of covenant. The tenant admits one late payment of service charges (subsequently remedied). Can the landlord succeed on this ground?

options.A: Yes — any breach is sufficient.
options.B: No — the ground requires persistent and substantial breaches; a single remedied breach is insufficient.
options.C: Yes — service charge breaches are automatically considered substantial.
options.D: No — service charge breaches are never grounds for opposition.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.30(1)(e) requires 'persistent substantial breaches' of covenant. A single isolated breach, even if later remedied, is unlikely to satisfy this test. The court has held that the breaches must be repeated, substantial in character, and show a pattern of non-compliance. One late payment of service charges would not constitute 'persistent' breaches sufficient to ground opposition.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Conflict of Interest — Buyer and Lender
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor is instructed to act for both buyer and lender in a purchase transaction. The buyer discloses that the property is being purchased at £100,000 below the lender's valuation. The lender has not been informed. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: Continue with the transaction and do not disclose the undervalue to the lender.
options.B: Immediately disclose the undervalue to the lender and obtain consent to proceed.
options.C: Advise the buyer to disclose the undervalue to the lender; if the buyer refuses, cease acting for the buyer.
options.D: Decline to act for the lender immediately and proceed only for the buyer.

Property Practice.explanation: The UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook specifically identifies undervalue purchases as creating a conflict of interest. The solicitor must: (1) immediately disclose the undervalue to the lender; (2) obtain the lender's express consent to proceed; and (3) if the lender does not consent or withdraws the mortgage offer, cease acting. Non-disclosure is a breach of the Handbook and could expose the solicitor to liability.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: In a chain of three property transactions (A→B, B→C, C→D), what is the critical requirement for simultaneous exchange of contracts?

options.A: All three contracts must be signed by the parties on the same calendar day.
options.B: Contracts must be exchanged in a coordinated manner so that each party becomes bound only when all parties in the chain are bound.
options.C: The middle party (B) must hold the contracts of all parties until release of all funds.
options.D: Exchange can occur individually; simultaneous exchange is not essential in a chain.

Property Practice.explanation: In a chain, simultaneous exchange is essential to protect each party's position. Contracts must be exchanged in a coordinated manner — typically via telephone with the cooperation of all solicitors — so that each party is bound only when all parties in the chain are committed. Failure to achieve simultaneous exchange exposes parties to risk (e.g., if the first transaction falls through, others may be unable to proceed). This is achieved through careful sequencing and telephone conversations releasing contracts together.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: During the requisitions on title phase, the buyer's solicitor discovers that a covenant affecting the property (not disclosed in the contract) permits only residential use, yet the property has been used for a small commercial office for five years. The seller produces no evidence of discharge or waiver. What is the position?

options.A: The buyer must complete; the covenant is presumed obsolete after five years of use.
options.B: The buyer is not entitled to rescind; the buyer can only claim damages.
options.C: This is a title defect; the buyer may rescind if the seller cannot remove the defect by producing a discharge or indemnity policy.
options.D: The seller is in breach of contract and must refund the purchase price.

Property Practice.explanation: A restrictive covenant is a defect in title if it remains binding and enforceable. Five years' non-enforcement does not discharge the covenant. The seller must either: (1) produce evidence of discharge (formal deed); (2) obtain a waiver from those with the benefit of the covenant; or (3) provide a restrictive covenant indemnity insurance policy. If the seller cannot remove the defect, the buyer is entitled to rescind unless agreeing to proceed subject to the defect and a price reduction.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor has submitted a SAR to the NCA regarding a client's property transaction. Before the NCA has responded, the client asks the solicitor why the transaction has not proceeded. Can the solicitor disclose to the client that an SAR has been submitted?

options.A: No — s.333A POCA 2002 prohibits 'tipping off' by disclosing that an SAR has been submitted, subject to statutory exceptions.
options.B: Yes — the client has a right to know why the transaction is delayed.
options.C: Yes — but only after receiving consent from the NCA.
options.D: No — but the solicitor can give a vague explanation of 'compliance issues' without naming the NCA.

Property Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.333A prohibits 'tipping off' — disclosure of the fact that an SAR has been submitted to the NCA if this would prejudice the NCA's investigation. This is a strict liability offence. The solicitor cannot disclose to the client that an SAR has been made, except where: (1) the NCA consents; (2) the disclosure is to a professional adviser for legal advice; or (3) the disclosure is to another financial institution for AML compliance purposes.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The seller fails to complete on the agreed completion date. Five days later, the buyer serves notice requiring completion within 5 working days. The seller still fails to complete. What is the buyer's primary remedy?

options.A: The buyer can only claim damages for breach of contract.
options.B: The buyer can rescind the contract and claim damages.
options.C: The buyer must complete the purchase even if the seller has failed to do so.
options.D: The buyer is entitled to specific performance of the contract.

Property Practice.explanation: If the seller fails to complete, the buyer's primary remedy is specific performance — an order requiring the seller to complete the sale. Damages are an inadequate remedy for breach of a unique asset (the property). The buyer serves notice to complete (giving 5+ working days); if the seller still fails, the buyer can pursue specific performance. Rescission is available but is a remedy of last resort; specific performance is the preferred remedy in property contracts.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor is acting for a trust (the purchaser). The trustee is known to the solicitor, but one beneficial owner's identity is unknown. What action must the solicitor take to comply with MLR 2017?

options.A: Proceed with the transaction; the MLR 2017 does not require identification of beneficial owners.
options.B: Obtain identity verification for the trustee and proceed; beneficial owners need not be verified.
options.C: Identify and verify the identity of all beneficial owners (those with 25%+ interest) before proceeding.
options.D: Obtain a trustee indemnity insurance policy instead of identifying beneficial owners.

Property Practice.explanation: MLR 2017 Regulation 5 requires Customer Due Diligence (CDD) for beneficial owners — persons who ultimately own or control 25% or more of the entity. If a trust is the purchaser, the solicitor must identify and verify the identity of all beneficial owners (trust beneficiaries with 25%+ interest). Failure to do so is a breach of AML obligations and may constitute failure to disclose money laundering.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A developer purchases Plot A for £2m and Plot B for £1.2m in separate but related transactions (linked transactions under FA 2003 s.108). The buyer intends to develop the two plots together. Are the purchases linked, and how should SDLT be calculated?

options.A: The transactions are not linked; SDLT is calculated separately on each property.
options.B: The transactions are linked; consideration is aggregated (£3.2m) for SDLT purposes, but Multiple Dwellings Relief (MDR) does not apply.
options.C: The transactions are linked; consideration is aggregated (£3.2m), and if the buyer is developing multiple dwellings, MDR may apply to reduce the rate.
options.D: Linked transactions always result in surcharge; SDLT is calculated at the highest rate on the aggregated consideration.

Property Practice.explanation: FA 2003 s.108 treats acquisitions of land within a certain period for the same or connected purposes as 'linked transactions.' The consideration is aggregated for SDLT purposes. If the acquisition is of land for development as multiple dwellings, Multiple Dwellings Relief (MDR) under FA 2003 Sch.6B may apply, reducing the SDLT rate from 5% to lower graduated rates. The solicitor must identify linkage and claim MDR if applicable.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A fraudster impersonates the registered owner and sells the property to an innocent buyer for value. The buyer registers the disposition. The true owner later discovers the fraud and seeks to recover the property. What is the priority position?

options.A: The true owner can recover; the buyer's registration is void because it is based on fraud.
options.B: The true owner can recover; an unregistered interest based on fraud cannot be overreached or overridden.
options.C: The buyer takes free of the true owner's interest under LRA 2002 s.29 because the buyer is a registered proprietor for value.
options.D: The buyer takes the legal estate but the true owner retains an overriding interest of actual occupation if in occupation.

Property Practice.explanation: This is the controversial position under LRA 2002 s.29: even if the registered proprietor obtained the property through fraud, a disposition by that proprietor to a buyer for value takes free of interests of any prior owner (subject to Sch.3 overriding interests). The true owner cannot recover the property from an innocent registered buyer, though they may have a claim against the fraudster for damages. This reflects the policy of LRA 2002 to protect purchasers of registered land.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A tenant and landlord enter into a commercial lease with a clause purporting to exclude LTA 1954 protection. The clause merely states 'This lease is excluded from the protection of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954.' Is this effective to contract out?

options.A: Yes — the clause clearly shows intention to contract out.
options.B: No — s.38A requires that the tenant be given statutory prescribed notice and must make a separate statutory declaration before or at the same time as entering into the lease.
options.C: Yes — but only if the lease is for a term of more than 14 years.
options.D: No — s.38A contracting out is now prohibited by recent statutory reform.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.38A(1)-(3) requires a strict procedural compliance for contracting out: (1) the landlord must serve a statutory prescribed notice on the tenant at least 14 days before the lease is entered; (2) the notice must comply with specific requirements (explain the effect of contracting out); and (3) the tenant must make a statutory declaration confirming they have received notice and understand the consequences. A mere clause in the lease is insufficient — the prescribed procedure is mandatory. Non-compliance means the tenant retains LTA 1954 protection.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Sch.3 Para 2 Overriding Interest vs Registered Charge
Property Practice.stem: A property is registered at HM Land Registry. The registered proprietor mortgages the property to Bank A and registers the charge. An unregistered interest based on actual occupation (Sch.3 para 2) arises before Bank A's charge is registered. What is the priority position?

options.A: The Sch.3 para 2 interest takes priority because it existed before the charge.
options.B: Bank A's registered charge takes priority because registration under LRA 2002 creates registered proprietor's interest.
options.C: Bank A's charge takes priority unless the holder of the Sch.3 interest was in occupation and visible to Bank A's surveyor.
options.D: The Sch.3 para 2 interest overrides the registered charge; priority is determined by order of acquisition, not registration.

Property Practice.explanation: A Sch.3 para 2 overriding interest (based on actual occupation) overrides ALL registered interests, including registered charges, regardless of when the charge is registered. If the person is in actual occupation and their interest is known to Bank A or would have been obvious on reasonable inspection, the interest overrides the charge registration. This reflects the principle that overriding interests 'trump' registered interests in priority. Bank A's remedy is against the valuer for negligence.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Property Fraud — Identity and Lender Indemnity
Property Practice.stem: A fraudster impersonates the true owner and instructs a solicitor to sell the property. The solicitor carries out identity checks but the fraudster provides forged identity documents. The property is sold to a lender as security. The lender later discovers the fraud and seeks an indemnity from the solicitor's professional indemnity insurer. What is likely the outcome?

options.A: The indemnity will be paid because the solicitor relied on documents provided by the fraudster.
options.B: The indemnity will be refused because the solicitor negligently failed to detect the fraud.
options.C: The indemnity will be paid only if the lender has a separate indemnity policy against fraud.
options.D: The indemnity will be paid in full because property fraud is a recognised risk covered by all policies.

Property Practice.explanation: Professional indemnity insurance typically excludes or limits cover for losses arising from fraudulent impersonation if the solicitor has failed to conduct proper identity verification. Modern PI policies include explicit fraud exclusions or require compliance with AML CDD procedures. If the solicitor accepted forged documents without scrutiny, the insurer may refuse the claim as a breach of policy conditions. The lender's remedy depends on whether they have a separate lender indemnity policy (which may also have exclusions for fraud by the borrower).

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Beneficial Ownership — Trust and Overreaching Failure
Property Practice.stem: A property is registered in the names of two registered proprietors (A and B) but the beneficial interest is held entirely by a third party, C, under a trust (only A and B are trustees). C is not in actual occupation. A and B sell the property to a buyer. Capital money is paid to A only. What is C's position?

options.A: C's beneficial interest is overreached because A and B are trustees, regardless of payment to one trustee only.
options.B: C's beneficial interest is NOT overreached because capital money must be paid to BOTH trustees (or a trust corporation).
options.C: C has an overriding interest under Sch.3 para 2 because C has a beneficial interest.
options.D: C's beneficial interest is overreached if A and B acted in good faith.

Property Practice.explanation: Overreaching under LPA 1925 ss.2 and 27 requires that capital money be paid to AT LEAST two trustees (or a trust corporation). Payment to A alone is insufficient — the capital must be paid to both A and B, or to one trustee and a trust corporation. Because overreaching has not been completed (capital paid to one trustee only), C's beneficial interest is NOT overreached and remains binding on the buyer. The buyer takes subject to C's interest and the buyer may be able to claim a charge against the purchase money in C's hands.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Alienation Covenant — s.19 LTA 1927 and 1995 Act
Property Practice.stem: A commercial lease contains an absolute covenant against assignment. The tenant wishes to assign to a proposed assignee with excellent covenant strength. Under LTA 1927 s.19, the landlord refuses consent arbitrarily (for reasons unrelated to the business use of the premises). Subsequently, the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 applies to the lease on later assignments. What is the legal position?

options.A: The covenant remains absolute; the landlord is not required to give consent under either Act.
options.B: LTA 1927 s.19(1) implies a term that landlord consent cannot be unreasonably withheld; the 1995 Act overrides this for later assignments.
options.C: LTA 1927 s.19(1) implies a term that consent cannot be unreasonably withheld; the 1995 Act applies similar protections but allows landlords to impose reasonable conditions.
options.D: The 1995 Act automatically discharges the absolute covenant, permitting all assignments.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1927 s.19(1) converts an absolute covenant against assignment into a qualified covenant — the landlord's consent cannot be unreasonably withheld (but conditions can be imposed). The Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 (applying to post-1996 leases or where the parties elect) provides similar protection: the landlord must give consent unless a reasonable ground exists. Grounds include: (1) unsuitability of the assignee; (2) potential deterioration of the premises; (3) security concerns. The landlord cannot refuse on arbitrary grounds unrelated to the lease.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo is purchasing a residential property in Wharton Magna. Her solicitor carries out a local authority search. The search reveals a financial charge on the property registered in the Local Land Charges Register. What is the significance of a local land charge?

options.A: Local land charges bind the owner but not a purchaser who completes without actual notice.
options.B: Local land charges bind all persons with an interest in the land, including purchasers, regardless of whether they have actual notice — they are overriding interests in registered land.
options.C: Local land charges are registrable only in the Land Registry and have no effect until registered.
options.D: A local land charge automatically ceases to bind the land on a sale to a purchaser for value.

Property Practice.explanation: Under the Local Land Charges Act 1975, local land charges (which include financial charges, planning obligations, smoke control orders etc.) bind all persons with an interest in the land, whether or not they have notice. They run with the land. A purchaser who completes is bound whether or not the charge appeared in a search (though the seller may be liable for non-disclosure). They are distinct from Land Registry entries; the LLC Register is maintained by the local authority (now digitised by HM Land Registry). Option A is wrong (no notice defence); C is wrong (different register); D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Gerald Crane is purchasing a commercial property in Wharton Magna. His solicitor is advising on what searches to conduct. The drainage search reveals that the public sewer runs within the boundaries of the property. Why is this significant?

options.A: It is purely informational — the owner may build over a public sewer without restriction.
options.B: Building over or near a public sewer is controlled by the sewerage undertaker; the presence of a public sewer within the property boundary may restrict future development and may require the undertaker's consent before building. This may affect the site's development potential.
options.C: A public sewer within the boundary automatically makes the land unregistrable.
options.D: The owner can apply to the water authority to have the sewer re-routed at no cost.

Property Practice.explanation: Water Industry Act 1991 and sewerage undertaker's Build Over Agreement procedures: building over or within a specified distance of a public sewer is controlled. Consent (a Build Over Agreement) is normally required. A sewer within the property boundary can significantly restrict development potential — affecting planning applications and building regulations. This is important due diligence for commercial purchases. Option A is wrong — there are restrictions; C and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Stamp Duty Land Tax — rates and thresholds
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari purchases a residential property in England for £400,000. It is his first residential property and his only home. What is his Stamp Duty Land Tax liability (applying the standard residential rates in force as of April 2025)?

options.A: £10,000 — a flat 2.5% on the purchase price.
options.B: £10,000 — calculated on the current standard SDLT thresholds: 0% on the first £250,000; 5% on the portion from £250,001 to £925,000; resulting in (£0 + £7,500) = £7,500. [Note: check current thresholds — correct calculation at current rates applies.]
options.D: Nil — first-time buyers are fully exempt up to £500,000.

Property Practice.explanation: SDLT for residential property uses a tiered rate structure under Finance Act 2003. At the standard residential rates (as of April 2025, following reverting from the temporary nil-rate threshold): 0% on the first £250,000; 5% on £250,001–£925,000. On a £400,000 purchase: 0% on £250,000 = £0; 5% on £150,000 = £7,500. Total = £7,500. First-time buyer relief may reduce this further (0% on first £425,000 for FTBs purchasing up to £625,000). Option B provides the standard non-FTB calculation; D overstates FTB relief. Students should apply current rates — the exam will test the tiered structure and correct application.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: James Ashworth already owns two residential properties. He purchases a further residential property for £500,000. What SDLT rate applies?

options.A: The standard residential SDLT rates apply — there is no additional charge for buying multiple properties.
options.B: An additional 3% surcharge (on top of standard residential rates) applies to the entire purchase price of each additional residential property purchased by an individual who already owns another residential property.
options.C: James pays commercial SDLT rates because he owns multiple properties.
options.D: Multiple dwellings relief automatically reduces the SDLT to nil.

Property Practice.explanation: Finance Act 2003 Sch 4ZA (Higher Rates for Additional Dwellings — HRAD) imposes a 3% surcharge on top of standard residential SDLT rates for purchasers who own another residential property (in England, Wales, N. Ireland or internationally) at the end of the day of completion. The surcharge applies to the entire purchase price. So James pays standard rates + 3% on each band. Option A ignores the surcharge; C applies commercial rates incorrectly; D — multiple dwellings relief (MDR) was abolished for completions on or after 1 June 2024.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo is purchasing 14 Millfield Lane, Wharton Magna, which is registered with absolute freehold title. What does absolute freehold title guarantee?

options.A: It guarantees that the land is free from all encumbrances and third-party rights.
options.B: Absolute freehold title is the best class of title; it means the registrar is satisfied with the title, giving the proprietor and their successors the greatest security. However, it does not guarantee freedom from overriding interests (Schedule 3 LRA 2002) or other registered encumbrances.
options.C: Absolute title means the land has never been subject to a mortgage.
options.D: Absolute title means the current owner has held the property for more than 15 years.

Property Practice.explanation: Under the Land Registration Act 2002, 'absolute freehold title' is the highest quality of registered title. Section 11 LRA 2002: the effect of first registration with absolute title is that the proprietor takes the estate free of other interests (subject to registered encumbrances and overriding interests). It does not guarantee freedom from overriding interests (Schedule 3 — e.g. short leases, legal easements, interests of persons in actual occupation). Option A is wrong — overriding interests bind; C and D introduce incorrect criteria.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Overriding interests — persons in actual occupation
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari is purchasing a property from Gerald Crane. Unknown to Hassan, Priya Mehta has been living in the property for two years and contributed to the purchase price. She has not registered any interest. Is Priya's interest binding on Hassan?

options.A: No — Priya's interest is not binding because it is not registered at the Land Registry.
options.B: Priya's interest may be an overriding interest under Schedule 3 paragraph 2 LRA 2002 (interest of person in actual occupation), binding Hassan if: she was in actual occupation at the time of the disposition, and her occupation would have been apparent on a reasonable inspection of the property OR Hassan had actual knowledge of it.
options.C: Priya's interest only binds Gerald as vendor; it cannot bind a purchaser.
options.D: Priya can only protect her interest by lodging a caution against first registration.

Property Practice.explanation: Schedule 3 paragraph 2 LRA 2002: an interest belonging to a person in actual occupation is an overriding interest (binding on purchasers without registration) unless: (i) inquiry was made of that person before the disposition and the interest was not disclosed; or (ii) the occupation was not apparent on reasonable inspection and the purchaser had no knowledge. Priya's actual occupation and beneficial interest (arising from constructive trust or resulting trust — Stack v Dowden [2007]) may override the registered title. Option A is wrong — overriding interests bind without registration; C is wrong; D relates to unregistered land.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Exchange of contracts — effect and risk
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo exchanges contracts to buy a residential property. The day before completion, the property is destroyed by fire. The property is not insured by Rachel. Who bears the risk of loss?

options.A: The risk remains with the seller until legal completion and the seller must reinstate or rescind.
options.B: Under the general law (Paine v Meller (1801)), once contracts are exchanged, the equitable interest passes to the buyer and risk passes to the buyer. Standard Conditions of Sale (5th ed) condition 5.1 modifies this — risk passes to the buyer on exchange unless the property is sold with vacant possession and the property is not insured by the seller.
options.C: Risk of loss is always shared equally between buyer and seller until legal completion.
options.D: Risk only passes on legal completion — exchange has no effect on risk.

Property Practice.explanation: At common law, on exchange of contracts the equitable interest passes and risk passes to the buyer (Paine v Meller [1801]). The Standard Conditions of Sale (5th edition) condition 5.1.1 modifies this: the property is at the seller's risk until completion IF the seller is obliged to transfer with vacant possession and the SCs apply. If the Standard Conditions are varied or not incorporated, common law applies. Solicitors should advise the buyer to insure from exchange. The precise outcome depends on whether the SCs apply — but the general rule is that common law risk passes on exchange.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Acting for Gerald Crane as purchaser of commercial premises, the solicitor raises requisitions on title with the seller's solicitor. What is the purpose of requisitions on title?

options.A: To renegotiate the purchase price after exchange of contracts.
options.B: To raise formal enquiries about outstanding matters arising from the title documents provided — for example, to query an unresolved charge, request redemption figures, or seek confirmation that obligations have been met — so that title is in good order for completion.
options.C: To notify the Land Registry of the pending transfer.
options.D: Requisitions on title are sent before exchange of contracts as part of pre-contract enquiries.

Property Practice.explanation: Requisitions on title are formal enquiries raised by the buyer's solicitor after receiving the title documents (usually after exchange in modern practice, though sometimes used pre-exchange). They identify outstanding issues: undischarged mortgages, outstanding notices, missing consents, requests for completion information. Standard requisitions (using the Law Society's completion information and undertakings form) also request undertakings as to discharge of mortgages on or before completion. Option A is wrong (not for price renegotiation); C describes Land Registry notification; D is incorrect as to timing.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Completion — undertakings as to mortgage discharge
Property Practice.stem: The seller of a property to Hassan Nazari has a registered charge (mortgage) on title. The seller's solicitor gives an undertaking to the buyer's solicitor to discharge the mortgage on completion. What are the legal consequences of a solicitor's undertaking?

options.A: An undertaking is a statement of intention but has no binding professional effect.
options.B: A solicitor's undertaking is personally binding on the solicitor and enforceable by the court as a matter of professional conduct. Failure to comply is a serious disciplinary and professional matter; the SRA may impose sanctions.
options.C: Undertakings are binding only on the firm, not on the individual solicitor.
options.D: Undertakings given at completion must be registered at the Land Registry to take effect.

Property Practice.explanation: A solicitor's undertaking is one of the most serious professional obligations. It is personally binding on the giving solicitor, not merely the firm (though the firm may also be liable). Courts have jurisdiction to enforce undertakings summarily (Re a Solicitor [1966]). Breach of undertaking is professional misconduct that may result in SRA investigation, conditions, suspension or strike-off. The mortgage discharge undertaking is one of the most common in residential conveyancing and must be scrupulously honoured. Option A is wrong; C is incorrect (personal liability exists); D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo completes the purchase of an unregistered freehold property. What must her solicitor do, and what are the consequences of delay?

options.A: Registration is optional for freehold properties — Rachel may hold the unregistered title deeds.
options.B: Rachel's solicitor must apply for first registration at the Land Registry within two months of completion (s.6 LRA 2002); if registration is not completed in time, the legal estate reverts to the seller on trust for Rachel as the equitable owner until registration is effected.
options.C: First registration must take place within six months; failure has no legal consequence but the Land Registry may impose a fine.
options.D: Unregistered freehold land in England and Wales does not require registration on sale.

Property Practice.explanation: Land Registration Act 2002 s.4: compulsory first registration is triggered by certain events including a qualifying transfer of an unregistered freehold. Section 6: the application must be made within two months of the triggering event. Section 7: if registration is not made within that period, the transfer becomes void and the legal estate reverts to the transferor, who holds it on a bare trust for the transferee. The transferee retains an equitable interest but loses the legal estate until registration occurs. Option A is wrong — registration is compulsory; C gets the time limit wrong; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering holds a 15-year commercial lease of premises in Wharton Magna. The lease contains an absolute prohibition on assignment. Hollowell wishes to assign the lease to Northgate Couriers. Can Hollowell assign without the landlord's consent?

options.A: Yes — an absolute prohibition on assignment is unenforceable under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1927.
options.B: No — an absolute prohibition means the tenant cannot assign at all, even with the landlord's consent; breach would entitle the landlord to forfeit the lease. Unlike qualified covenants (which the LTA 1988 governs), an absolute prohibition requires the landlord's agreement to vary the covenant before any assignment can occur.
options.C: Yes — Hollowell may assign to a company in the same group without needing consent.
options.D: The landlord must consent to any assignment provided Northgate Couriers can demonstrate financial standing.

Property Practice.explanation: Alienation covenants may be absolute (no assignment ever) or qualified (not without consent). The Landlord and Tenant Act 1988 (reasonableness obligation) only applies to qualified covenants — it requires landlords not to unreasonably withhold consent. An absolute covenant means the tenant cannot assign at all; even consent does not usually override it without a deed of variation. The Landlord and Tenant Act 1927 s.19 implies a proviso of reasonableness into qualified (not absolute) covenants. Option A wrongly applies LTA 1927 to an absolute prohibition; C introduces a group company exception not applicable here; D describes a qualified covenant.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier takes a full repairing and insuring (FRI) lease of offices. At the end of the term, the landlord serves a schedule of dilapidations claiming the cost of repairs. Under which principle is the landlord's claim capped?

options.A: The claim is uncapped — the tenant must pay all costs in the schedule of dilapidations regardless of amount.
options.B: Under s.18(1) Landlord and Tenant Act 1927, damages for breach of a repairing covenant are capped at the diminution in the value of the reversion — the landlord cannot recover more than the reduction in the value of their interest caused by the disrepair.
options.C: The tenant's liability is limited to the original cost of construction of the repairs.
options.D: The cap applies only where the landlord intends to demolish the property.

Property Practice.explanation: Section 18(1) Landlord and Tenant Act 1927 imposes two limits: (i) damages for breach of a repairing covenant cannot exceed the diminution in value of the reversion caused by the breach; and (ii) damages are nil if the premises are, or would at or shortly after expiry of the term be, pulled down or altered so that the repairs would be valueless. The cap protects tenants from excessive claims where the landlord intends to redevelop. Option A is wrong — there is a statutory cap; C and D misstate the test.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering falls into arrears of rent on its commercial lease. The landlord, Ashworth Developments Ltd, peacefully re-enters the premises and changes the locks without court proceedings. Is this lawful, and what may Hollowell do?

options.A: The re-entry is always unlawful; the landlord must obtain a court order before forfeiting any lease.
options.B: For non-residential premises, peaceful re-entry without court proceedings may be a lawful method of forfeiture if the tenant is not in occupation. Hollowell may apply to the court for relief from forfeiture under s.146(2) LPA 1925 (or s.38 SCA 1981) — the court has a discretion to grant relief, usually restoring the lease on terms including payment of arrears.
options.C: Once the landlord has peacefully re-entered, Hollowell has no right to relief from forfeiture.
options.D: Relief from forfeiture is only available to mortgagees, not to tenants.

Property Practice.explanation: Peaceful re-entry (without court process) is a recognised method of forfeiture for non-residential premises (it carries criminal law risks under s.6 Criminal Law Act 1977 if persons are in occupation). For commercial premises where there is no residential element, peaceful re-entry may be lawful. However, the tenant retains the right to apply for relief from forfeiture under s.146(2) LPA 1925 (for breach of covenant other than non-payment of rent) or under inherent equitable jurisdiction / s.38 Senior Courts Act 1981 (for non-payment of rent). Courts regularly grant relief on terms including payment of arrears and costs. Option A is wrong for non-residential; C is wrong — relief is available; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Gerald Crane instructs Crane & Pelletier to act on the purchase of a commercial property for £2.5m. The purchase is being funded partly by an overseas company. What AML obligations does the firm have at the outset?

options.A: AML obligations only apply to residential conveyancing transactions; commercial property is exempt.
options.B: The firm must conduct client due diligence (CDD) under the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017: verify the client's identity, identify and verify the beneficial owners of the overseas company, understand the source of funds for the transaction, and assess the risk of money laundering.
options.C: The firm need only conduct CDD if the purchase price exceeds £5m.
options.D: CDD is required only when the solicitor suspects money laundering, not on every transaction.

Property Practice.explanation: The MLR 2017 require regulated firms (including solicitors conducting property transactions) to apply CDD at the outset of a business relationship or occasional transaction. CDD includes: (i) identifying and verifying the client; (ii) identifying and verifying beneficial owners (Reg 28 — 25%+ ownership or control threshold for companies); (iii) understanding the nature and purpose of the transaction and source of funds. Enhanced due diligence (EDD) applies to high-risk situations (e.g. PEPs, high-value transactions with overseas entities). Option A is wrong; C and D introduce incorrect thresholds/triggers.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: During the conveyancing process, Rachel Okonkwo's solicitor discovers that the source of funds cannot be satisfactorily explained and has reason to suspect the funds may be the proceeds of crime. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: Immediately disclose the suspicion to the client and refuse to proceed.
options.B: The solicitor must submit a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency via the Authorised Disclosure route under POCA 2002 s.338 before proceeding with the transaction; the solicitor must not 'tip off' the client (s.333A POCA) and must await appropriate consent (or the expiry of the moratorium period) before completing.
options.C: Refuse to act without giving reasons; no reporting obligation arises unless the solicitor has proof of money laundering.
options.D: Report the suspicion to the Land Registry to prevent registration of the transaction.

Property Practice.explanation: Solicitors in the regulated sector committing a 'regulated sector offence' (transacting with property they know or suspect to be criminal property) must make an authorised disclosure to the NCA before completing (POCA 2002 s.338 — the 'consent SAR'). The tipping off prohibition (s.333A) means the solicitor cannot warn the client. The NCA has 7 working days to refuse consent (or 31 calendar days if a moratorium is requested). The solicitor must not complete until consent is given or the moratorium expires. Option A — tipping off — is prohibited; C is wrong on the trigger (suspicion suffices); D is wrong (no role for the Land Registry).

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The transfer deed for the purchase by Hassan Nazari states that Gerald Crane transfers the property 'with full title guarantee'. What implied covenants arise under the Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1994?

options.A: A full title guarantee implies that the seller is the beneficial owner and the property is free from all encumbrances.
options.B: A full title guarantee implies: (i) the seller has the right to dispose; (ii) the seller will do further acts to perfect title if needed; (iii) the property is free from incumbrances other than those of which the seller is unaware and could not reasonably be expected to know; and (iv) easements and rights benefiting the property are included.
options.C: Full title guarantee means the seller warrants that the property is free from all mortgages and charges currently registered.
options.D: Full title guarantee is equivalent to an absolute guarantee of title free from all third-party claims.

Property Practice.explanation: The Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1994 provides that 'full title guarantee' implies specific covenants: (i) right to dispose (s.2); (ii) further assurance (s.2(3)); (iii) freedom from incumbrances, except those the seller did not know about and could not reasonably be expected to know (s.3). It does NOT guarantee freedom from all incumbrances, only from those within the seller's knowledge. Limited title guarantee implies only the right to dispose and further assurance — not the incumbrance covenant. Option A and D overstate the guarantee; C limits it to mortgages.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Business tenancy — Landlord and Tenant Act 1954
Property Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers occupies commercial premises under a lease protected by the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954. The landlord, Ashworth Developments Ltd, serves a s.25 notice terminating the lease and opposing renewal under ground (f) — intention to redevelop. What must the landlord demonstrate?

options.A: That it has planning permission — no other evidence is required.
options.B: That at the date of the hearing, the landlord has a firm and settled intention to carry out substantial works of demolition or reconstruction of the holding, and that the works cannot reasonably be carried out without obtaining possession.
options.C: That the tenant has breached the lease, which entitles the landlord to possession for redevelopment.
options.D: Ground (f) is absolute — once asserted in a s.25 notice, the court must grant possession.

Property Practice.explanation: Ground (f) LTA 1954 s.30(1)(f): the landlord intends to demolish or reconstruct the premises or a substantial part of them, or carry out substantial works of construction, and cannot reasonably do so without obtaining vacant possession. The landlord must prove: (i) genuine intention at the hearing date (objective evidence — plans, planning applications, building contracts etc. — Gregson v Cyril Lord [1963]); (ii) the works are substantial; (iii) possession is necessary for their execution. Option A sets too low a bar; C introduces tenant breach (ground (b)); D is wrong — the tenant may argue the ground is not made out.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Acting for Rachel Okonkwo as buyer of a residential property at £350,000, the solicitor prepares a completion financial statement. Which items are normally included?

options.A: The completion financial statement accounts for all monies: purchase price less deposit already paid; mortgage advance; SDLT; Land Registry fees; solicitor's fees and disbursements; search costs; and any apportionments (e.g. ground rent, service charge) — giving the net amount the buyer must provide.
options.B: Only the purchase price and mortgage advance are relevant.
options.C: The financial statement is prepared by the mortgage lender, not the solicitor.
options.D: A financial statement is only required for commercial transactions above £1m.

Property Practice.explanation: The solicitor's financial statement to the buyer accounts for all financial aspects of the transaction: the balance of the purchase price (after deducting the deposit already paid), the mortgage advance (deducted from the amount required), SDLT payable, Land Registry registration fees, legal fees (plus VAT), disbursements (searches, Land Registry searches), and any adjustments for apportionments of outgoings. This gives the client the precise sum needed to proceed to completion. Options A, C and D are all incorrect.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The registered title for a property being purchased by Gerald Crane shows a restriction in Form A: 'No disposition of the registered estate by the proprietor of the registered estate is to be registered without a certificate signed by the applicant for registration or their conveyancer that the provisions of section 2 of the Trusts of Land and Appointment of Trustees Act 1996 have been complied with.' What does this mean in practice?

options.A: The property is held on a trust of land; two or more trustees (or a trust corporation) must execute the transfer so that the purchaser takes free of the beneficial interests under the doctrine of overreaching. The solicitor must confirm TLATA 1996 s.2 compliance (two trustee capital money receipt).
options.B: The property cannot be transferred without a court order.
options.C: The restriction prevents any sale of the property during the seller's lifetime.
options.D: The restriction means the seller must obtain the consent of a named third party before selling.

Property Practice.explanation: A Form A restriction is entered where property is held on a trust of land. Section 2 TLATA 1996 enables overreaching — where capital money arising from a disposition is paid to at least two trustees (or a trust corporation), beneficial interests under the trust are overreached and purchasers take free of them (Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981] distinguished; City of London Building Society v Flegg [1988] applied). The restriction ensures the two-trustee requirement is satisfied on transfer. Option B requires a court order, which is not the case; C and D describe different types of restriction.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A conveyancing search on a property being purchased by Hassan Nazari reveals a risk of chancel repair liability. What is this, and how can it be addressed?

options.A: Chancel repair liability is an ancient obligation that may require the owner of certain land to contribute to the cost of repairing the chancel of the local parish church. Following Wallbank v Parochial Church Council of Aston Cantlow [2004], it can bind purchasers as an overriding interest if registered. It is addressed by obtaining chancel repair liability indemnity insurance.
options.B: Chancel repair liability is a form of planning obligation and can be discharged by paying the local authority.
options.C: Chancel repair liability was abolished by the Land Registration Act 2002 and no longer affects registered land.
options.D: Chancel repair liability only arises in leasehold properties, not freeholds.

Property Practice.explanation: Chancel repair liability is a historic obligation to contribute to the cost of repairs to the chancel (the area around the altar) of a Church of England parish church. Following Wallbank v PCC of Aston Cantlow [2004] HL, the obligation can be enforced against lay rectors' successors in title. The LRA 2002 s.117 removed the overriding interest status of unregistered chancel liabilities after October 2013, but registered notices can still bind purchasers. The practical solution is indemnity insurance. Option C is partially correct on unregistered liabilities but incorrect as a general statement; A and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Covenants — benefit and burden in freehold land
Property Practice.stem: A restrictive covenant was imposed on land now owned by Ashworth Developments Ltd in 1985, preventing use for commercial purposes. Ashworth now proposes to build a warehouse. The benefit of the covenant was annexed to adjacent land now owned by Ravi Sharma. Can Ravi enforce the covenant?

options.A: Ravi may enforce the restrictive covenant if: the burden has passed to Ashworth (under Tulk v Moxhay [1848] — it must have been intended to run, relate to land, Ashworth must have had notice, and the covenant must be negative); and the benefit has passed to Ravi (by annexation, assignment or a building scheme).
options.B: No — restrictive covenants between predecessors in title cannot be enforced by or against successors.
options.C: Only the original covenantee (the person who received the covenant in 1985) can enforce it.
options.D: The covenant cannot be enforced after 20 years have passed.

Property Practice.explanation: Restrictive covenants in freehold land bind successors if the burden and benefit have both passed. Burden: Tulk v Moxhay [1848] — the covenant must (i) be negative/restrictive; (ii) be intended to bind successors (touch and concern the land); (iii) the covenantor's successors must have notice (registered as a land charge in unregistered land or entered in the charges register in registered land). Benefit: passes by express annexation, statutory annexation (LPA 1925 s.78), assignment or building scheme (Federated Homes [1980]). All conditions appear satisfied on these facts. Option B is wrong; C ignores benefit/burden principles; D introduces an incorrect time limit.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier's commercial lease includes a service charge clause requiring payment of a fair proportion of the costs of maintaining and repairing common parts of a Wharton Magna office building. The landlord carries out works costing £500,000 to replace the roof. Is Crane & Pelletier liable for its proportion?

options.A: Liability depends on the drafting of the service charge clause. If the clause covers the relevant works (e.g. major structural repairs/renewal rather than just routine maintenance), Crane & Pelletier will be liable for its proportion. Courts construe service charge clauses strictly, and works not within the defined scope may not be recoverable.
options.B: Yes, regardless of the circumstances — service charge clauses are enforceable for all works.
options.C: Service charges in commercial leases are limited to 10% of annual rent by statute.
options.D: Crane & Pelletier can refuse to pay if the works were not authorised by all tenants.

Property Practice.explanation: Commercial service charge liability depends on the lease drafting. Unlike residential service charges (which are regulated by the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985), commercial service charges are largely governed by contract. Courts construe service charge clauses strictly (Holding & Management Ltd v Property Holding & Investment Trust plc [1989]). Works must fall within the scope of the clause — 'maintenance and repair' clauses may not extend to full replacement if the roof has exceeded its useful life. The RICS Code of Practice on Service Charges provides guidance. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari's property is subject to two registered charges in favour of different lenders. The first charge was registered before the second. The property is sold on default. In what order are the lenders paid?

options.A: Priority of registered charges over the same registered estate is determined by the order in which they were registered (s.48 LRA 2002), not the date of creation. The first registered charge takes priority; the second charge takes what remains.
options.B: The order is determined by the date of the loan agreement, not the date of registration.
options.C: Both charges rank equally and the proceeds are shared pro rata.
options.D: Unsecured creditors take priority over registered charges on insolvency.

Property Practice.explanation: Section 48(1) Land Registration Act 2002: charges over the same registered estate have priority according to the order in which they are entered in the register. The first charge (registered first) takes full priority; the second charge takes only what remains after the first is fully satisfied. This gives commercial certainty — lenders can rely on the register. Option B is wrong — registration date governs; C is wrong — there is a priority order; D is wrong — secured creditors take priority over unsecured creditors on insolvency.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Conflict of interest — acting for buyer and lender
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier's residential conveyancing team is instructed by both Rachel Okonkwo (buyer) and her mortgage lender on the same transaction. During the transaction, the solicitor discovers that Rachel has omitted to tell the lender that she is receiving a gifted deposit from a family member. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: The solicitor must advise Rachel that the information must be disclosed to the lender, and if Rachel refuses to consent to disclosure, the solicitor must cease to act for both Rachel and the lender; the solicitor cannot complete the transaction without the lender being fully informed of the gifted deposit.
options.B: Ignore the omission — it is not the solicitor's responsibility to report client information to the lender.
options.C: Disclose the information to the lender without Rachel's consent, as the duty to the lender overrides client confidentiality.
options.D: Report only to the lender and continue acting for Rachel without telling her.

Property Practice.explanation: When acting for buyer and lender, the solicitor owes duties to both. A gifted deposit is material information the lender requires (SRA Code of Conduct; CML/UK Finance Lenders Handbook obligations). If Rachel refuses to allow disclosure, there is a conflict between duties to client (confidentiality) and to lender (disclosure). The solicitor must: advise Rachel to consent to disclosure; if she refuses, withdraw from acting for both parties, as the solicitor cannot assist a transaction that involves misleading the lender. Option B breaches duties to the lender; C breaches confidentiality without consent; D is an inconsistent approach.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari is purchasing a newly built house from Ashworth Developments Ltd. The developer offers a 10-year Buildmark warranty from the NHBC. What does this cover during the initial period?

options.A: The NHBC Buildmark warranty provides: (i) a builder's period (first 2 years) during which the builder is responsible for putting right defects in compliance with NHBC standards; and (ii) a structural insurance period (years 3-10) covering major structural defects. The scheme also covers insolvency of the developer before completion.
options.B: The Buildmark warranty is purely cosmetic — it only covers paint defects.
options.C: The Buildmark warranty covers all defects discovered at any point in the first 10 years.
options.D: The warranty only applies if Hassan is the original purchaser and cannot be assigned to future buyers.

Property Practice.explanation: The NHBC Buildmark warranty operates in two phases: (i) the initial two-year period ('builder's period') — the developer/builder must remedy defects arising from failure to comply with NHBC standards; (ii) years 3-10 — NHBC structural defect insurance covers major structural defects if the builder fails to remedy them. The warranty also provides cover if the developer becomes insolvent before completion. It does not cover all defects in years 3-10 (only structural). The warranty is transferable to subsequent buyers. Option B is wrong; C overstates coverage; D is wrong — the warranty is transferable.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Ground rent — Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo is purchasing a new long residential lease. The developer proposes a ground rent of £250 per annum doubling every 10 years. Is this lawful for new leases under current legislation?

options.A: No — the Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022 prohibits 'prohibited rent' in regulated leases granted on or after 30 June 2022. The permitted ground rent is restricted to zero (a 'peppercorn'). A £250 doubling rent would be a prohibited rent, the landlord committing an offence.
options.B: Yes — ground rents in residential leases are unrestricted provided they are disclosed in the lease.
options.C: Ground rents are lawful up to £250 per annum but the doubling clause would need to be removed.
options.D: The restriction on ground rents applies only to leases over 200 years.

Property Practice.explanation: The Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022 applies to 'regulated leases' (new long residential leases of dwellings granted on or after 30 June 2022). For regulated leases, the 'restricted rent' is a peppercorn — effectively zero. Any ground rent higher than a peppercorn is a 'prohibited rent'. Landlords who charge prohibited rents commit an offence (maximum £5,000 fine). Options A, C and D misstate the 2022 Act's effect.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Stamp Duty Land Tax — commercial rates
Property Practice.stem: Gerald Crane purchases a commercial property in Wharton Magna for £1.5m. What are the applicable SDLT rates for commercial/non-residential property?

options.A: Non-residential SDLT rates apply: 0% on the first £150,000; 2% on £150,001–£250,000; 5% on the portion above £250,000. On a £1.5m purchase: (0% × £150k) + (2% × £100k) + (5% × £1.25m) = £0 + £2,000 + £62,500 = £64,500.
options.B: The standard residential SDLT rates apply with an additional 3% surcharge.
options.C: A flat 5% applies to the entire purchase price of commercial property.
options.D: No SDLT is payable on commercial property purchases.

Property Practice.explanation: SDLT for non-residential/mixed-use property uses separate rate bands: 0% on the first £150,000; 2% on £150,001–£250,000; 5% on amounts above £250,000 (Finance Act 2003 as amended). On a £1.5m purchase: £150k × 0% = £0; £100k × 2% = £2,000; £1.25m × 5% = £62,500. Total = £64,500. The additional 3% HRAD surcharge does not apply to commercial property. The 3% (now 5% for additional residential dwellings) applies only to additional residential dwellings. Option B is wrong; C and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering is purchasing a former industrial site. An environmental search reveals potential contamination from a previous use as a chemical plant. What is the buyer's primary legal risk and how should it be addressed in due diligence?

options.A: Under Part IIA Environmental Protection Act 1990, the current owner/occupier of contaminated land may be liable for remediation if the original polluter cannot be found. The buyer should commission a Phase I/II environmental survey, consider appropriate contractual protections (warranties, price adjustment, remediation obligations on the seller), and assess whether the liability can be insured.
options.B: The buyer has no liability for contamination from a previous owner's activities.
options.C: Environmental liability for industrial contamination is always passed to the local authority on sale.
options.D: The Limitation Act 1980 bars all contamination claims after 6 years.

Property Practice.explanation: Part IIA Environmental Protection Act 1990 imposes liability for remediation of contaminated land on 'Class A' persons (the polluter) and, if the polluter cannot be found, on 'Class B' persons (current owners/occupiers). A purchaser of contaminated land may inherit remediation liability. Thorough due diligence — Phase I (desk-based) and Phase II (intrusive surveys) — is essential. Legal protections include: contractual warranties as to contamination, price reduction, seller undertaking remediation, or environmental indemnity insurance. Option B is wrong; C is wrong; D does not apply to statutory contamination liability.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The seller of a house (Victoria Ashworth) completes a TA6 Seller's Property Information form and fails to disclose a neighbour dispute that has been ongoing for 18 months. Rachel Okonkwo purchases the property and later suffers loss because of the dispute. What claims may Rachel have?

options.A: Victoria may be liable for misrepresentation (by omission where there is a duty to disclose): under the Misrepresentation Act 1967, if the non-disclosure amounts to a false representation of fact, Rachel may claim damages and/or rescission. The TA6 expressly asks about disputes — failing to disclose is likely a fraudulent or negligent misrepresentation.
options.B: Rachel has no claim — the caveat emptor principle means buyers take the risk of undisclosed matters.
options.C: Rachel may only claim if she proves Victoria's non-disclosure was fraudulent.
options.D: The solicitor's search of the local authority register would have revealed the dispute.

Property Practice.explanation: The TA6 Property Information Form expressly asks the seller about disputes with neighbours. A false or misleading answer (including an omission where the form creates a false impression) may give rise to liability for misrepresentation under the Misrepresentation Act 1967. If the misrepresentation is fraudulent (Derry v Peek [1889]) or negligent (s.2(1) MA 1967), the buyer may claim damages and potentially rescission. Option B — caveat emptor — has been significantly qualified in modern conveyancing by mandatory disclosure obligations in the TA6. Option C is wrong — negligent misrepresentation suffices under s.2(1). Option D is incorrect — neighbour disputes are not revealed by local authority searches.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Solicitor acting for seller and buyer — conflict of interest
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor at Crane & Pelletier is asked to act for both Gerald Crane (seller) and Hassan Nazari (buyer) in a residential property transaction. Under the SRA Code of Conduct 2019, when may a solicitor act for both parties?

options.A: The SRA Code (paragraph 6.2) prohibits acting where there is a conflict of interest or a significant risk of conflict.
options.B: A solicitor may always act for buyer and seller in the same transaction provided both clients give written consent.
