options.C: A solicitor may act for buyer and seller provided they are in the same family.
options.D: Acting for both is permissible provided a different fee earner acts for each party within the same firm.

Property Practice.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct 2019 paragraphs 6.2 and 6.3 restrict acting where there is a conflict of interest or significant risk of conflict. In conveyancing, buyer and seller have inherently opposed interests. The SRA's position (and the previous practice rules) is that acting for both buyer and seller in a residential transaction is generally prohibited unless: the transaction is a standard residential one and the parties have been given independent advice about acting jointly, or it is a simple remortgage. Acting for both buyer and lender is expressly addressed in the CML Handbook/UK Finance Lenders' Handbook. Options A and D are too permissive; C introduces an irrelevant family criterion.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo is purchasing a long residential leasehold flat. Which aspects of the lease must her solicitor investigate on her behalf?

options.A: The solicitor must investigate: length of unexpired term (fewer than 80 years may affect mortgageability and enfranchisement rights); ground rent provisions (Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022); service charge obligations and management; repair covenants; alienation restrictions; user clauses; insurance obligations; and any forfeiture provisions — advising on all material terms.
options.B: Only the length of the term remaining — all other lease terms are standard.
options.C: The solicitor investigates only the ground rent and service charge.
options.D: Leasehold title is guaranteed by absolute title registration — no further investigation is needed.

Property Practice.explanation: Investigating a long residential lease requires comprehensive review: (i) unexpired term (under 80 years affects mortgage availability and the premium for statutory lease extension — LTA 1993; under 70 years many lenders decline); (ii) ground rent (Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022 for post-June 2022 leases); (iii) service charge provisions and management compliance; (iv) repair covenants; (v) alienation restrictions (assignment/subletting); (vi) user clause; (vii) insurance; (viii) forfeiture clauses. Absolute title does not guarantee the lease terms are favourable. Options A, C and D understate the investigation required.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Lease extension — statutory right under LTA 1993
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo has owned her long leasehold flat for 3 years and the lease has 72 years remaining. She wishes to extend it statutorily. Which requirements must she satisfy under the Leasehold Reform, Housing and Urban Development Act 1993?

options.A: She may extend without conditions — any long leaseholder may extend at any time.
options.B: She must have owned the lease for at least 5 years before making a claim.
options.C: Under the LTA 1993 Part I Chapter II, Rachel must: (i) hold a long lease (exceeding 21 years) of a flat; (ii) have owned the lease for at least 2 years. If entitled, she may claim a statutory lease extension of 90 years added to the unexpired term at a peppercorn rent, paying a premium calculated actuarially.
options.D: Statutory lease extension is only available where the unexpired term is below 50 years.

Property Practice.explanation: Leasehold Reform, Housing and Urban Development Act 1993 s.39: a qualifying tenant of a flat is entitled to claim a new lease. Qualifying conditions: (i) long leasehold of a flat (originally granted for more than 21 years); (ii) the tenant must have owned the leasehold estate for at least 2 years (s.39(2)). Rachel has owned for 3 years — satisfied. The new lease adds 90 years to the unexpired term at a peppercorn ground rent. A premium is payable calculated under Schedule 13 (deferment rate, relativity etc.). Option B states 5 years (wrong — it is 2 years); D imposes a threshold not in the Act; A removes the 2-year qualification.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Business tenancy — security of tenure and contracting out
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is about to sign a 5-year commercial lease. The landlord insists on a 'contracted-out' lease excluding the security of tenure provisions of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954. What procedure must be followed for the contracting out to be valid?

options.A: The parties can exclude the LTA 1954 simply by inserting a clause in the lease to that effect.
options.B: Contracting out requires court approval in all cases.
options.C: Under the LTA 1954 s.38A and the Regulatory Reform (Business Tenancies) (England and Wales) Order 2003, the landlord must serve a prescribed warning notice on the tenant before the tenant enters into the lease or an agreement for it
options.D: Contracting out is not possible for leases of 5 years or more.

Property Practice.explanation: Section 38A LTA 1954 (as amended by the RRO 2003) permits contracting out of security of tenure without court approval, provided the correct procedure is followed. The landlord must serve a warning notice in the prescribed form on the tenant not less than 14 days before the tenant enters the lease/agreement (or the tenant may make a statutory declaration if fewer than 14 days). The tenant (or their authorised person) must make a simple declaration (if 14+ days' notice) or statutory declaration (if less). Both must happen before the lease is completed. Option A is wrong — a mere clause is ineffective; C describes the pre-2003 procedure; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The solicitor acting for Hassan Nazari in his purchase is also acting for his mortgage lender. The solicitor discovers that Hassan intends to sublet the property immediately after completion without telling the lender, contrary to the mortgage conditions. What should the solicitor do?

options.A: Complete the transaction and note the subletting intention on file for future reference.
options.B: The solicitor should report the intention directly to the lender without telling Hassan.
options.C: The solicitor should advise Hassan that the intention to sublet immediately must be disclosed to the lender, as it is material information affecting the mortgage. If Hassan refuses to consent to disclosure, the solicitor must cease acting for both the lender and Hassan, as the solicitor cannot assist a transaction that would involve misleading the lender.
options.D: The lender's mortgage conditions are the lender's concern, not the solicitor's.

Property Practice.explanation: Acting for buyer and lender, the solicitor owes duties to both. Under the UK Finance Lenders' Handbook, the solicitor must report to the lender any matter that may affect the lender's decision to proceed. An intention to sublet contrary to the mortgage conditions is material — lenders grant lower LTV mortgages on buy-to-let bases with different conditions. Concealing this from the lender would involve the solicitor in assisting a fraud on the lender. The solicitor must advise disclosure; if refused, must withdraw from acting for both (SRA Code para 6.2). Option B breaches confidentiality without consent; A and D ignore the duty to the lender.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Gerald Crane is purchasing unregistered land. The title shows a 1998 restrictive covenant registered as a Class D(ii) Land Charge. Gerald's solicitor fails to search the Land Charges Register. Gerald completes the purchase. Is Gerald bound by the covenant?

options.A: No — Gerald is not bound because he had no actual notice of the covenant.
options.B: Gerald is only bound if he had actual or constructive notice of the covenant.
options.C: Yes — under the Land Charges Act 1972, a properly registered Class D(ii) land charge (restrictive covenant) is binding on a purchaser for money or money's worth regardless of notice; failure to search does not excuse the purchaser.
options.D: The covenant is unenforceable because it was registered more than 20 years before the purchase.

Property Practice.explanation: Land Charges Act 1972 s.4(6): a Class D(ii) land charge (restrictive covenant entered into after 1925) is void against a purchaser of a legal estate for money or money's worth only if it is not registered. If it IS registered, it binds all purchasers regardless of notice (that is the entire point of the registration system — notice is irrelevant). Conversely, if it were not registered, it would be void against the purchaser for money or money's worth. On these facts, it is registered — it binds Gerald regardless of search failure. Option A applies the notice doctrine, which is displaced by the Land Charges Act; C is wrong; D introduces an incorrect time limit.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd is selling a commercial property and has 'opted to tax' (elected for VAT) over the property. Gerald Crane is the purchaser. What is the VAT effect on the transaction?

options.A: There is no VAT on commercial property sales regardless of any election.
options.B: The opt to tax means the buyer must register for VAT before completing the purchase.
options.C: Where the seller has opted to tax and the option is valid, the sale of the property becomes a standard-rated supply for VAT purposes, and VAT (at 20%) is charged on the purchase price. The buyer may recover the VAT as input tax if they are VAT-registered and using the property for taxable business purposes, or the parties may qualify for a Transfer of a Going Concern (TOGC) treatment.
options.D: VAT on commercial property is at the reduced rate of 5%.

Property Practice.explanation: The election to waive exemption (option to tax) under VATA 1994 Sch 10 converts what would be an exempt supply (commercial land and buildings) into a standard-rated supply (20% VAT). If the seller has opted to tax, VAT is added to the purchase price. The buyer can recover the VAT as input tax if VAT-registered and using the property for taxable purposes. Transfer of a Going Concern (TOGC) rules may apply if certain conditions are met, making the supply outside the scope of VAT. This is important due diligence in commercial transactions. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is in negotiations to take a new commercial lease of office premises. Heads of terms have been agreed. What is the legal status of heads of terms?

options.A: Heads of terms are legally binding once signed — either party may sue for breach if they withdraw from the deal.
options.B: Heads of terms automatically become binding if the parties continue to negotiate for more than 30 days.
options.C: Heads of terms are generally 'subject to contract' and therefore not legally binding — they record the commercial terms agreed in principle for incorporation in the formal lease, but either party may withdraw without liability unless specific binding obligations are expressly included.
options.D: Heads of terms are binding in commercial property transactions but not in residential transactions.

Property Practice.explanation: Heads of terms in commercial property transactions are usually expressed to be 'subject to contract' — they are therefore not legally binding (Winn v Bull [1877]; Regalian Properties v London Docklands Development Corporation [1995]). They record the commercial agreement in principle pending the formal documentation. Either party may withdraw without liability for breach of contract. Some specific elements of heads of terms may be binding (e.g. exclusivity/lock-out agreements, confidentiality), if expressly agreed. Option A is wrong — 'subject to contract' negates binding effect; C introduces an arbitrary trigger; D has no legal basis.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Grant of easement — express and implied
Property Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd sells part of its Wharton Magna land to Rachel Okonkwo without expressly granting a right of way over the retained land, which is the only reasonable means of access to Rachel's plot. Can Rachel claim an easement by implication?

options.A: No — easements must always be expressly granted in the transfer deed; implied easements do not exist in registered land.
options.B: Rachel must apply to the tribunal for a statutory right of way under s.26 LRA 2002.
options.C: Yes — under the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows (1879), quasi-easements that are continuous and apparent and reasonably necessary for reasonable enjoyment of the property may pass on a grant. Additionally, an easement of necessity may be implied if the land is landlocked. Under s.62 LPA 1925, rights enjoyed over adjoining land may pass automatically on a conveyance.
options.D: An implied easement can only be acquired by 20 years' use under the doctrine of prescription.

Property Practice.explanation: Implied easements arise in several ways: (i) Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] — quasi-easements continuous, apparent and necessary for reasonable enjoyment pass on a grant of part; (ii) easement of necessity — implied where without it the grantee's land would be inaccessible; (iii) common intention — where parties intended the land to be used in a way requiring the easement; (iv) s.62 LPA 1925 — rights, privileges and advantages enjoyed with the land prior to conveyance may pass as easements. The fact there is no other access strongly supports an easement of necessity. Option A is wrong — implied easements exist in registered land; C refers to a statutory provision that is not the primary route; D refers to prescription.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari purchases two adjacent residential properties from Ashworth Developments Ltd as part of a single negotiation and scheme, completing on the same day for £400,000 and £350,000 respectively. Are the transactions linked for SDLT?

options.A: No — each transaction is separate for SDLT purposes and SDLT is calculated independently on each price.
options.B: Linked transaction rules only apply to commercial property purchases.
options.C: Under FA 2003 s.108, transactions are 'linked' if they form part of a single scheme, arrangement or series between the same buyer and seller. Linked transactions are treated as a single transaction: SDLT is calculated on the aggregate consideration (£750,000) and then apportioned between the properties. This typically results in a higher effective rate.
options.D: SDLT linked transactions are only relevant where the buyer is a company.

Property Practice.explanation: Finance Act 2003 s.108: transactions are linked if they are part of a single scheme, arrangement or series between the same vendor and purchaser (or their connected persons). Linked transactions are treated as a single transaction for SDLT — the rates are applied to the aggregate consideration and the liability is apportioned. On £750,000 aggregate, the effective rate is higher than on each property individually. This prevents artificial structuring. Options A, C and D misstate the linked transactions rules.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Rachel Okonkwo has agreed (subject to contract) to buy a property at £450,000. Before exchange, the seller accepts a higher offer from another buyer (gazumping). Has the seller acted unlawfully, and could a lock-out agreement have helped?

options.A: Yes — gazumping is unlawful because it breaches the agreement to sell to Rachel.
options.B: Rachel can claim damages under the Misrepresentation Act 1967 for the seller's change of mind.
options.C: No — because the agreement was 'subject to contract', no binding contract existed. The seller is free to accept another offer. A lock-out agreement (binding the seller not to negotiate with other buyers for a defined period) could have given Rachel a contractual remedy for that period if properly drafted and supported by consideration.
options.D: An estate agent owes Rachel a duty not to accept higher offers on the seller's behalf.

Property Practice.explanation: In English law, property transactions are 'subject to contract' until exchange — no binding contract exists until exchange of contracts. Gazumping (accepting a higher offer before exchange) is not unlawful (Walford v Miles [1992] — no general obligation to negotiate in good faith). A lock-out agreement — an agreement by the seller not to negotiate with other buyers for a specified period — is enforceable if supported by consideration and certain in duration (Pitt v PHH Asset Management [1994]). It gives Rachel a contractual claim during the exclusivity period. Option A is wrong — no contract exists; C is wrong — no actionable misrepresentation; D is wrong — estate agents owe duties to the seller.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The solicitor acting for Gerald Crane on the purchase of industrial premises near a former mining area is advised to obtain a coal authority search. What does this search reveal?

options.A: The presence of previous owners' debts secured on the property.
options.B: The planning history of the site only.
options.C: Whether the property is affected by past, present or proposed coal mining activity, including areas of subsidence risk, mine entries, and proposed underground works — relevant to structural risk and insurance.
options.D: Whether the property is connected to the national gas grid.

Property Practice.explanation: A coal authority search (available from the Coal Authority) identifies: (i) whether the property is in an area where mining has taken place, is taking place, or is proposed; (ii) the location of known mine entries; (iii) areas of surface hazard risk. In mining areas, properties may be at risk of subsidence from historical extraction. This affects structural insurance, mortgage availability, and development viability. It is essential due diligence in former mining areas. Options A, C and D describe entirely different searches.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier's solicitors need to transfer completion funds to the seller's solicitors for completion of a residential purchase. What is the standard mechanism, and what time risk must the solicitor manage?

options.A: Completion funds are transferred by cheque; the seller must wait for clearance.
options.B: Completion funds may be transferred by BACS which takes three days; the parties agree to waive the time risk.
options.C: Completion funds are transferred by CHAPS (Clearing House Automated Payment System), which provides same-day cleared funds. The solicitor must ensure funds are sent in sufficient time to arrive before the agreed completion time — late arrival can result in contractual penalties, interest, and deferral of completion.
options.D: Funds are held by the Land Registry and released on registration.

Property Practice.explanation: The standard method of transferring completion funds in conveyancing is CHAPS, which provides same-day guaranteed cleared funds. The solicitor must ensure funds are sent early enough to reach the seller's solicitor's account by the agreed completion time (often 1 or 2 pm). If funds arrive late, the Standard Conditions of Sale (SCS 6.1) provide that the buyer has not completed on time, attracting interest and potentially allowing the seller to serve a notice to complete. BACS (3 days) is inappropriate; cheques are not used; the Land Registry does not hold funds.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Agreement for lease — conditions and long-stop
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering enters an agreement for lease of new offices to be constructed by Ashworth Developments Ltd. Completion is conditional on planning permission being obtained by a specified long-stop date. Planning is refused and the long-stop date passes. What is the position?

options.A: The agreement for lease automatically converts to a binding lease on the long-stop date.
options.B: Hollowell must continue to wait — planning can always be re-applied for.
options.C: If the agreement for lease provides that it becomes void if planning is not obtained by the long-stop date, both parties are released from their obligations. If the agreement is silent, the position depends on construction of the agreement — typically either party may rescind if the condition has failed without fault.
options.D: The failure of a planning condition is a repudiatory breach by the developer entitling Hollowell to damages.

Property Practice.explanation: Agreements for lease are commonly conditional on planning permission being obtained. Standard drafting provides that if the condition fails by the long-stop date, the agreement is void and both parties are released. If the agreement does not address this scenario, the parties must look to general contract law — the condition may be a condition precedent to the agreement, or one party may have assumed responsibility for obtaining permission. The outcome depends on the drafting. Option A is wrong; C does not address the legal position; D only applies if the developer was in breach of an obligation to use reasonable endeavours to obtain planning permission.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari is declared bankrupt after exchanging but before completing on a property purchase. What is the effect on the purchase contract and the solicitor's position?

options.A: The contract is automatically rescinded on Hassan's bankruptcy, releasing the seller.
options.B: Hassan's bankruptcy has no effect on completion — the sale must proceed as contracted.
options.C: On bankruptcy, Hassan's estate (including his interest under the contract) vests in the trustee in bankruptcy (Insolvency Act 1986 s.306). The trustee may disclaim the contract as onerous, or proceed with it if it is beneficial. The seller's solicitor should be advised, and completion should not proceed without the trustee's involvement and authority.
options.D: The seller may immediately rescind and retain the deposit on bankruptcy.

Property Practice.explanation: Insolvency Act 1986 s.306: on a bankruptcy order, the bankrupt's estate vests automatically in the trustee in bankruptcy. This includes equitable interests under contracts, including the interest arising on exchange of a purchase contract. The trustee may disclaim onerous property (s.315 IA 1986) or, if the transaction is beneficial to the estate, may complete. The seller's solicitor should be notified and no completion should occur without the trustee's consent. Option A is wrong — the contract does not automatically rescind; C ignores the trustee's role; D overstates the seller's rights (specific provisions of the contract and general law apply).

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier's commercial lease contains a tenant's break clause exercisable after 5 years, provided the tenant gives 6 months' notice and is not in breach of its obligations at the break date. Crane & Pelletier gives notice but has an outstanding rent arrear of £500 on the break date. Is the break effective?

options.A: Yes — a minor arrear does not invalidate a break clause.
options.B: The landlord must elect whether to waive the breach before the break date.
options.C: The effectiveness of the break clause depends on the drafting. If the clause requires the tenant to be in full compliance or free from all breaches, the courts have applied strict compliance: even a minor technical breach may prevent the break from taking effect (Riverside Park Ltd v NHS Property Services Ltd [2016]; Siemens Hearing Instruments Ltd v Friends Life Ltd [2014]).
options.D: Break clauses are always effective regardless of technical breaches.

Property Practice.explanation: English courts have applied strict compliance to break clause conditions — if the condition requires no material breach or no outstanding obligations, courts construe this strictly. In Siemens Hearing Instruments [2014] and Riverside Park [2016], even minor arrears or technical breaches prevented break clauses from taking effect. The practical consequence is that tenants must audit all obligations before a break date and ensure no arrears or breaches exist. The drafting of the particular clause is determinative. Option A underestimates the risk; C describes a different issue; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Gerald Crane is purchasing a property in an area potentially affected by a proposed national infrastructure project. What searches should his solicitor consider beyond the standard suite?

options.A: No additional searches are needed — the local authority search covers all infrastructure projects.
options.B: The solicitor should only search if the client requests it.
options.C: Infrastructure projects are a matter of general knowledge; no searches are needed.
options.D: For properties in areas affected by major infrastructure projects (such as HS2), the solicitor should consider: HS2 searches (land within the Act limits, safeguarding zones); Infrastructure Planning searches for development consent orders; and the Planning Inspectorate's Nationally Significant Infrastructure Project register — as these affect development potential, compensation rights and value.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard local authority searches may not reveal all infrastructure planning impacts. HS2 searches (or equivalent for other nationally significant infrastructure projects) are specifically recommended for affected areas: they identify whether land is within the Act limits (compulsory acquisition zones), safeguarding areas (affecting planning applications), or compensation entitlement zones. Failure to advise on appropriate specialist searches in known infrastructure corridors could constitute negligence. Options A, C and D inadequately protect the client's interests.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Alek Kowalski has been in adverse possession of a strip of registered land bordering his property for 12 years. The registered owner, Ashworth Developments Ltd, discovers the occupation. What is the position under the Land Registration Act 2002?

options.A: After 12 years of adverse possession, Alek automatically acquires the legal title to the strip.
options.B: Adverse possession of registered land is impossible — the registered owner cannot lose title to a squatter.
options.C: The 12-year limitation period that applied to unregistered land applies equally to registered land under the LRA 2002.
options.D: Under the LRA 2002 schedule 6, after 10 years of adverse possession, Alek may apply to be registered as proprietor. The Land Registry notifies the registered owner, who has 65 business days to oppose. If Ashworth opposes, Alek's application is rejected but Alek has a further 2 years to bring further proceedings. If Ashworth fails to respond, Alek is registered.

Property Practice.explanation: The LRA 2002 radically changed adverse possession for registered land. Schedule 6: after 10 years of adverse possession, the squatter may apply for registration. The Land Registry serves notice on the registered proprietor (and others). If the proprietor (or mortgagee) objects within 65 business days, the application fails — but if the squatter remains for a further 2 years without the proprietor regularising the position, the squatter may apply again and will be registered. Option A applies the old Limitation Act 1980 rule (12 years for unregistered land); C is wrong for registered land; D is wrong — adverse possession can succeed under the LRA 2002 procedure.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering's commercial lease contains an upward-only rent review clause providing for rent to be reviewed to the open market rent every 5 years. At the review date, the open market rent has fallen. What is the effect?

options.A: The rent reduces to the current market rent — the clause must be applied at the open market rate.
options.B: The landlord must accept the lower market rent if they wish to retain the tenant.
options.C: An upward-only clause is automatically void under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.
options.D: Under an upward-only rent review clause, the rent on review cannot fall below the existing passing rent — if the open market rent is lower, the passing rent is maintained at its current level. The clause is designed to protect the landlord from market falls.

Property Practice.explanation: Upward-only rent review clauses are standard in commercial leases. They provide that on review, the revised rent will be the higher of: the passing rent or the open market rent. Where the market has fallen, the rent stays at the existing level (it cannot fall). They are not unlawful (attempts to restrict them by statute have not been implemented). The Government's 2004 Code of Practice for Commercial Leases recommends offering alternatives, but upward-only clauses remain enforceable. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Completion of Hassan Nazari's purchase is due today. The seller's solicitor has received the funds but the seller refuses to vacate the property. Hassan's solicitor wishes to take action. What formal step can be taken and with what consequence?

options.A: Hassan can immediately rescind the contract and claim all losses.
options.B: The solicitor must apply to the court for specific performance immediately on the completion date.
options.C: Hassan must wait indefinitely for the seller to vacate before taking any further steps.
options.D: Under the Standard Conditions of Sale condition 6.8, a party not in default may serve a notice to complete giving the other party 10 working days to complete. If the defaulting party fails to complete within that period, the serving party may rescind the contract — the buyer recovering the deposit (with interest) and damages for breach.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard Conditions of Sale (5th edition) condition 6.8: if the transaction is not completed on the contractual completion date, either party not in default may serve a notice to complete making time of the essence. The notice gives 10 working days. If the seller fails to complete: the buyer may rescind, recover the deposit (and interest) and sue for damages. Specific performance (D) is available as an alternative but is a slower court process. Option A is wrong — the notice procedure must first be followed under the SCs before rescission; C is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: At completion of a residential sale, the seller's solicitor at Crane & Pelletier gives an undertaking to the buyer's solicitor to use the proceeds of sale to discharge the seller's mortgage and to forward evidence of discharge within a specified period. The proceeds are received but the solicitor inadvertently uses them to offset a firm debt. The mortgage is not discharged. What are the professional consequences?

options.A: The solicitor has made a minor administrative error with no serious professional consequences.
options.B: The solicitor's obligation under the undertaking terminates if the mortgage lender refuses to provide a redemption statement.
options.C: The buyer's solicitor must apply to court to enforce the undertaking — no professional conduct action arises.
options.D: Breach of a solicitor's undertaking is a serious professional conduct matter. The SRA may investigate for breach of SRA Principles (acting with integrity, honesty and in clients' best interests) and breach of the SRA Accounts Rules (misuse of client…

Property Practice.explanation: A solicitor's undertaking is one of the most serious professional obligations. Breach exposes the giving solicitor to: (i) professional conduct proceedings before the SRA (Principles 2, 4, 5 — integrity, honesty, proper supervision); (ii) potential SRA intervention; (iii) disciplinary sanction up to strike-off; (iv) personal liability to the buyer's solicitor. Using completion proceeds to offset a firm debt is a misuse of client money under the SRA Accounts Rules. Courts can also enforce undertakings summarily. Options A, C and D all dramatically understate the seriousness of the breach.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd is letting commercial premises to Northgate Couriers. What are the seller/landlord's obligations in relation to an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) and the Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards (MEES)?

options.A: EPCs are only required for residential property; commercial lettings are exempt.
options.B: The tenant, not the landlord, is responsible for obtaining an EPC on taking a commercial lease.
options.C: EPC requirements only apply to buildings over 1,000 square metres.
options.D: The Energy Performance of Buildings (England and Wales) Regulations 2012 require a valid EPC to be provided to prospective commercial tenants.

Property Practice.explanation: EPCs are required on the construction, sale or letting of most buildings, including commercial properties, under the 2012 Regulations. MEES (currently minimum E rating for commercial property) prohibit granting new lettings or continuing existing lettings of sub-standard commercial premises unless an exemption applies (e.g. third party consent refused, devaluation, or wall/floor insulation exemption). The EPC must be provided to prospective tenants. Government policy is to raise the minimum standard further. Option A is wrong; C has no basis; D is wrong — the landlord must provide.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is conducting a residential conveyancing transaction and is required to carry out electronic verification of identity for both the client and any third parties. Under what framework does this now operate?

options.A: Electronic identity verification is prohibited in conveyancing — all identity checks must be done in person.
options.B: Electronic verification of identity is mandatory and face-to-face checks are no longer permitted.
options.C: Electronic identity checks are sufficient only for purchases under £200,000.
options.D: HM Land Registry's Digital Identity Standard (and the associated Practice Guide 67) provides a framework for electronic identity verification using Identification Documents Validation Technology (IDVT). From 2023, the Land Registry accepts digital identity checks for conveyancing purposes, provided they meet the required standard. The solicitor must document the process.

Property Practice.explanation: HM Land Registry Practice Guide 67 and the Land Registry's Digital Identity Standard (introduced from April 2022 onwards) provide a framework allowing conveyancers to use certified Identity Service Providers to verify identity digitally to the required standard (complying with DIATF — UK Digital Identity and Attributes Trust Framework). This satisfies both AML/CDD and Land Registry identity verification requirements. The solicitor must retain records of the verification process. Option A is wrong; C introduces a false financial limit; D is wrong — face-to-face remains acceptable.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Positive covenants — burden not passing at common law
Property Practice.stem: A previous owner of a freehold property covenanted to maintain a boundary wall shared with an adjacent property owned by Ravi Sharma. The property has since been sold to Gerald Crane. Ravi seeks to enforce the obligation against Gerald. Is Gerald bound?

options.A: Yes — the burden of all covenants runs with freehold land in the same way as restrictive covenants.
options.B: Positive covenants are enforced by the local authority under planning law.
options.C: Gerald is bound because the covenant was registered at the Land Registry.
options.D: No — the burden of a positive covenant (one requiring expenditure or action) does not run at law or in equity with freehold land to bind successors (Halsall v Brizell [1957] and Rhone v Stephens [1994]). Ravi can only sue Gerald's predecessor. Gerald is not bound unless there is a chain of indemnity covenants, a Halsall v Brizell mutual benefit and burden, or an estate rentcharge.

Property Practice.explanation: The fundamental principle in English law (confirmed by the House of Lords in Rhone v Stephens [1994]) is that the burden of a positive freehold covenant does not pass with the land — subsequent owners of the burdened land are not automatically bound. The only mechanisms to enforce positive obligations against successors are: (i) a chain of indemnity covenants (personal contract right only); (ii) Halsall v Brizell mutual benefit and burden (if Gerald takes benefit of the covenant scheme, he must take the burden); (iii) estate rentcharge; (iv) commonhold. Registration at the Land Registry does not make a positive covenant binding against successors. Option A is wrong; C is wrong; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers' commercial tenancy of Wharton Magna premises is protected by the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954. The landlord does not oppose renewal. The parties cannot agree on the new rent. How is the rent determined?

options.A: The rent is fixed at the level agreed in the previous lease with a 10% increase.
options.B: If the parties cannot agree, the tenancy continues on the old terms indefinitely.
options.C: The landlord's last asking rent is automatically accepted if the parties cannot agree.
options.D: On an unopposed renewal under LTA 1954, if the parties cannot agree the rent, the County Court sets it at the open market rent, disregarding the tenant's improvements and goodwill.

Property Practice.explanation: Section 34 LTA 1954 provides the formula for rent on renewal: the open market rent, assessed on a willing landlord/willing tenant basis at the date of commencement of the new tenancy. Three disregards apply (s.34(1)): (i) the tenant's occupation; (ii) goodwill attaching to the holding; (iii) improvements carried out by the tenant (subject to conditions). This prevents the landlord from capturing the tenant's own investment in the premises. Option A introduces an arbitrary increase; C gives the landlord unilateral power; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Capacity — acting for a client with diminished capacity
Property Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier is instructed by Colonel Ashworth (aged 88) to sell his home. The solicitor meets Colonel Ashworth and forms concerns about his mental capacity to give instructions. What should the solicitor do?

options.A: Proceed with the transaction — the client is of legal age and must be presumed to have capacity.
options.B: Capacity is irrelevant in property transactions — the solicitor must follow the client's instructions.
options.C: The solicitor should immediately refer Colonel Ashworth to the Court of Protection without further discussion.
options.D: The solicitor should assess whether Colonel Ashworth has the mental capacity to give instructions for the transaction under the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

Property Practice.explanation: Under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (MCA), there is a presumption of capacity (s.1(2)), but if the solicitor has reasonable grounds to doubt capacity, they must conduct a capacity assessment (s.3 MCA — a person lacks capacity if they cannot understand, retain, use/weigh information or communicate a decision). The SRA Code requires solicitors to consider client vulnerability (SRA Guidance on the Vulnerability of Clients 2020). A solicitor should not proceed if capacity is in serious doubt. If capacity is lacking, a deputy/LPA attorney must instruct instead. An immediate Court of Protection referral (C) is premature and not the first step. Option A ignores professional obligations; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering assigns its commercial lease to Northgate Couriers with the landlord's consent. The landlord requires Hollowell to enter into an Authorised Guarantee Agreement (AGA). What does an AGA oblige Hollowell to do?

options.A: An AGA requires Hollowell to guarantee all existing obligations under the lease for its entire remaining term.
options.B: AGAs were abolished by the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 and are no longer used.
options.C: An AGA requires Hollowell to indemnify the landlord for any losses regardless of who is in occupation.
options.D: Under the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 s.16, the outgoing tenant may be required by the landlord as a condition of consent to assignment to enter an AGA, guaranteeing the performance of the lease obligations by the immediate assignee (Northgate Couriers) only — not by subsequent assignees. The AGA obligation ends if Northgate Couriers itself assigns the lease.

Property Practice.explanation: Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 s.16 permits landlords to require outgoing tenants to enter an Authorised Guarantee Agreement as a condition of consent to assignment. The AGA makes the outgoing tenant (Hollowell) a guarantor of the immediate assignee's (Northgate Couriers') performance of the lease covenants. The AGA is limited to guaranteeing the immediate assignee — it expires when that assignee in turn assigns (the former tenant cannot be required to guarantee indefinitely or down the chain). This is a material limitation compared to the pre-1995 Act privity of contract rule. Options A and C overstate the duration/scope; D is wrong — AGAs were introduced, not abolished, by the 1995 Act.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Twelve of the 20 long leaseholders of a Wharton Magna residential block wish to collectively enfranchise (purchase the freehold). Can they proceed under the Leasehold Reform, Housing and Urban Development Act 1993?

options.A: Collective enfranchisement requires all leaseholders in the block to participate.
options.B: Enfranchisement requires a court order and the freeholder's consent.
options.C: Collective enfranchisement is only available to blocks with fewer than 10 flats.
options.D: Under the LRHUDA 1993 Part I Chapter I, the participating tenants must comprise at least half of the total number of flats in the building (i.e. at least 10 of 20 — which is satisfied here). Each participant must be a qualifying tenant (long lease of a flat). The nominee purchaser serves an initial notice and must pay a market premium calculated under Schedule 6.

Property Practice.explanation: LRHUDA 1993 Part I: collective enfranchisement allows qualifying tenants of a self-contained building to purchase the freehold collectively. Requirements include: (i) at least two-thirds of the flats in the building must be held on long leases (the '2/3 flats' test — s.3); (ii) at least 50% of all qualifying tenants in the building must participate (the 'participation' threshold — s.13). With 12 of 20 participating, both thresholds are met (more than half). The nominee purchaser serves an initial notice (s.13 notice); the freeholder may serve a counter-notice. Option A wrongly requires unanimity; C is wrong; D wrongly requires consent.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Mortgagee's power of sale — s.101 LPA 1925
Property Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd has defaulted on its commercial mortgage. The lender wishes to sell the property without going to court. Under LPA 1925 ss.101 and 103, which condition must be satisfied before the statutory power of sale is exercisable (not merely arisen)?

options.A: The lender may exercise the power of sale as soon as the mortgage deed is executed
options.B: The power is only exercisable after the lender has obtained a court possession order
options.C: The power is only exercisable once the entire mortgage debt has been formally demanded and remains unpaid for 6 months
options.D: The power arises when the legal date of redemption has passed; it becomes exercisable when one of the s.103 conditions is met — such as a 3-month default after written notice, 2 months' interest arrears, or breach of a mortgage covenant

Property Practice.explanation: LPA 1925 s.101 implies a power of sale into mortgages made by deed (unless excluded) — this power arises when the mortgage money has become due (the legal redemption date has passed, typically 6 months after grant). Section 103 provides that the power is not exercisable (i.e. cannot actually be exercised) unless one of three conditions is satisfied: (i) notice requiring payment has been served and the mortgagor has defaulted for 3 months after that notice; or (ii) some interest under the mortgage is in arrears for 2 months; or (iii) there has been a breach of some provision contained in the mortgage deed other than the covenant to repay the principal sum or pay interest. All three are alternative conditions. No court order is required for an LPA s.101 sale.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd sells a development site to Hollowell Engineering with an overage clause providing that if planning permission for a significant uplift in use is obtained within 10 years, Ashworth will receive 25% of any uplift in value. How must this overage obligation be protected to bind subsequent purchasers of the site?

options.A: An overage clause is automatically binding on all purchasers once it is included in the transfer deed.
options.B: Overage clauses cannot be protected in registered land.
options.C: Overage obligations are enforceable as equitable interests without any registration.
options.D: To bind subsequent purchasers, the overage obligation (which is personal to Hollowell as purchaser unless properly protected) must be secured by one or more of: registration of a restriction in the Land Registry (limiting registration of subsequent…

Property Practice.explanation: Overage (clawback) clauses in property transactions are contractual obligations on the buyer. To ensure they bind successors (future purchasers of the site), the seller must register appropriate protection in the Land Registry: (i) a restriction preventing future disposals without the seller's consent or a certificate; (ii) a unilateral notice on the charges register; or (iii) a legal charge as security for the overage payment obligation. Overage obligations that are purely personal to the original buyer are not automatically binding on successors — the LRA 2002 priority rules apply. Options A and C are wrong; D is wrong.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Gifts from clients — undue influence risk
Property Practice.stem: Colonel Ashworth (aged 88) instructs Crane & Pelletier to draft a deed of gift transferring his house to his carer, Pat Hollowell, with no payment. The solicitor is aware that Pat is present at all meetings and Colonel Ashworth appears reluctant to speak freely. What professional obligations arise?

options.A: The solicitor should proceed — the client is an adult and has given instructions.
options.B: Provided Colonel Ashworth signs the deed, the solicitor's duty is satisfied.
options.C: The solicitor should immediately report Pat Hollowell to the police for elder abuse.
options.D: The solicitor must be alert to the risk of undue influence and potential abuse of a vulnerable client.

Property Practice.explanation: SRA Code of Conduct 2019 (principles of acting in the client's best interests, honesty and integrity) and the SRA Guidance on Vulnerable Clients require solicitors to consider undue influence where an elderly or vulnerable client proposes a substantial gift — particularly to a person in a position of trust (carer). The solicitor must: (i) see the client alone; (ii) assess capacity (MCA 2005); (iii) consider whether instructions are freely given; (iv) advise on independent legal advice; (v) potentially decline if the risk of undue influence is too high. An immediate police report (C) is premature — the solicitor should first address the professional obligations. Option A ignores vulnerability; D is insufficient.
